title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
research_problem: Exploring how various factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersection areas using data collected from Hefei, China
method_summary: A questionnaire was designed to collect pedestrians' socioeconomic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, a binomial logistic regression model was established using the Score test for variable selection to evaluate the impacts of contributing factors on pedestrians' red-light running behavior.
datasets: 631 valid survey samples collected from six signalized intersections in Hefei, China during May-June 2015
tasks: Identify factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
metrics: Score test, odds ratio, p-values, 95% confidence intervals
baselines: Reference categories: trip purpose (go to work/school), time period (dawn), gender (female), age (<12 years)

main_claims.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose, time period in a day, pedestrian's attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and pedestrian's attitude towards quality of road facility affecting crossing behavior
Strolling pedestrians are more likely to have safe crossing behavior compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio = 0.394)
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, dusk, and night periods
Pedestrians who indicate they will run a red light when in hurry are more likely to actually do so (odds ratio = 0.299)
Pedestrians whose crossing behavior is affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red lights at intersections without monitoring equipment

limitations.text: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not be exactly the same in other cities or countries due to differences in culture and driving behaviors
Study only considered pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersection areas, excluding other crossing facility types
Study focused on individual crossing behaviors while ignoring the impacts of pedestrian platoons
Sample size limited to 631 respondents from six intersections; more data from multiple intersections in other cities would strengthen findings

future_work: Collect data at multiple intersections in other Chinese cities to validate findings
Identify how pedestrians' red-light running behaviors are affected by various factors at other types of crossing facilities (e.g., midblock crossings with/without crosswalk buttons)
Study the impacts of pedestrian platoons on individual crossing behaviors
Compare results with studies from other countries to identify how cultural and driving factors affect differences in red-light running behaviors
notes: The study addresses a critical traffic safety issue in China where pedestrian-involved traffic crashes account for a quarter of all traffic deaths. The binomial logistic model uses Score test for variable selection at 95% confidence level. Key finding: pedestrians' attitudes (not just socioeconomic factors) significantly influence red-light running behavior.
